In general, the safer pattern is to initialize with `columns=None` and `if columns is None: columns = []` in the function body. This ensures that `columns`, if not provided, is always the empty list every function call.

In this case, I think we can probably leave it as it was originally (separate if-else blocks), or have something like:
```python
columns = [] if columns is None else columns

after hashing it out with @lloeki, we narrow the issue into that the that state of RC client and the state or the products could be out of sync after the fork.

For example:
1. a new configuration was in a process of been applied but didn't complete before the fork. so the client will not have on it state but it was applied by the process.
2. a configuration was removed from the state but product instrumentation was not yet removed before the fork.

In order to ensure that the rc-client state is the same the product state we need to one of two solutions:
1. **empty-state after fork** -  the rc-client after fork should notify all products to "remove all applied configurations" and then report an empty state to rc.
2. **ensure-current-state after fork** - the rc-client would notify all process on the "current" state on which configuration are applied. if the product see the configuration already applied, do nothing.

I'm not sure that it will be possible to implement overrides correctly after already having expanded parameters at the `BUILD` file parsing level. I had started out implementing #14408 this way (part of the reason why it and #14377 ended up so large!), but the challenge is that an override can prevent parametrization from occurring... and if it has already happened at `BUILD` parse time, it's too late to undo it.

As an example, the definition:
```python
python_sources(
    ..,
    resolve=parametrize('a', 'b'),
    overrides={'source1.py': {'resolve': "c"}},
)
```
...should result in only one copy of the target for `source1.py`. But if parametrization is applied at `BUILD` parse time, then at target generation time, there are two generator targets. That has two problems:
1.  Each of the generators would try to generate a target for `source1.py`, causing a collision
2. The generated target for `source1.py` would need to _remove_ parametrization from the address of the generator target, while generating since the `resolve` field was no longer `parametrized`.

The above approach will fail in one case. When two subprocess are started simultaneously (see example below). In that case, both subprocesses will be able to see `6379` as an available port and hence both will be using the same port despite the fact that the cause of the issue is usage of same port by multiple instances. So there are two ways in my mind to deal with this,

1. Select initial port as `6379` on a chance basis. For example, `port = default_port if random.random() >= 0.5 else default_port + 1`.
2. Or break from the `while num_choices > 0` loop on a chance basis so that only one of the two simultaneously running instances is able to grab `6379` as the first port.

The concept of type intersection does not exist in mypy and other type checkers. Maybe because there is usually two alternatives that better fits the same purpose:
1) either you formally want to merge the functional logic of two parents class: then you can use the Python multi-inheritance system (and so use the dedicated class hierarchy formed by this inheritance as types)
2) or you want to structurally validate the type with duck typing: then you can define a `Protocol` that defines the contract of methods a class must conform with, and use this protocol as type (eg. `Iterable` with `__iter__()` or `Sized` with `__len__()`)

Given that two classes (`ApacheConfigurator` and `NginxConfigurator`) effectively merge the functionalities of `interfaces.Authenticator`  and `common.Installer`, I think it is worth to follow 1) and define a dedicated abstract class that does that, and use it as type in the `ChallengePerformer`.

The benefit of moving this to the kernel level is that kernels are completely self-contained with the entire logic they need. Also adding the code there means that JIT users will be able to access the entire logic of the kernel which is great. Another benefit is that we keep the dispatch code VERY minimal without any custom logic. This could allow us to completely eliminate method bodies as discussed above.

The only drawback of pushing the entire code in the kernels is that for images, where we will have to support PIL, we would need to have that extra code on the kernel (the verification mostly). But from what I can see so far, that's a reasonable compromise for keeping this layer as thin as possible.

If you see other drawbacks it's worth discussing them. Otherwise I would be OK moving this to the kernel.

mode should default to `air_displacement` for `air` `default` or `none` liquid class types; and `positive_displacement` for `viscous` liquid class type. Currently, we don't specify what mode to use, because `air_displacement` is being hard-coded to be used. However, a user should be able to specify `mode` if needed, and if not specified, we should be able to resolve `mode` to whatever it fits... e.g. if a user wants to transfer glycerol, it should default to using `positive_displacement`, unless specified otherwise. This `mode` method allows for automatic resolution of mode depending on the liquid_class. Also, if say, glycerol is 'viscous' type, but if diluted enough, can be transferred using `air_displacement`. Hence, this will kick in only `if mode is None`. If user has a `mode` specified, that should be respected. Also, if (by any chance) there are mutiple `mode` specified in one liquid handle, that should be flagged... The behavior could also depend on how we add such aliquot propeties(?), tip types (since air-displacement and positive-displacement will use different tip types), etc, in the future as well....

If I did not define my own `from_params`, `Initializer`'s is called, it pops the type "pretrained" and calls `PretrainedModelInitializer`'s method, which does not exist, so it actually calls itself. Then, when `cls` is `PretrainedModelInitializer`, before it pops the type from the params, it first calls `cls.list_available()`. Within this method, it checks to ensure that the default implementation is registered. Since the default implementation for `PretrainedModelInitializer` does not exist, it uses `Initializer`, which is "normal". But because this is not registered for `PretrainedModelInitializer`, it raises a ConfigurationError [here](https://github.com/allenai/allennlp/blob/193bb04ddae7d89ee63005b898a01641447d6074/allennlp/common/registrable.py#L71).

I suggested `len()` because the data in a DataFrame should be one-dimensional, where each value is a record (a struct, a row of the table). However, checking if it's two dimensional is tricky. If a user supplies a list of lists, that should be valid, assuming the internal lists fit the column types. List of tuples should also be acceptable. If all the columns have the same datatype, a 2D array might also work. Regardless, the length of the data (if any are provided) should be used for the initial size of the underlying dataset. Any second dimension will be converted to the column data.

As for the None case, data should be optional eventually. It should be possible to create an empty DataFrame the same way it's possible to create an empty DataArray and append values to it.

When the list_blobs will be replaced with walk_blobs with a `delimiter='/'` parameter, the return items will be the contents of the directory. So if the directory structure is like so:
```
.zgroup
foo/.zgroup
foo/bar/.zarray
```
then `walk_blobs(self.container, name_starts_with='foo/', delimiter='/')` will return `['foo/.zgroup', 'foo/bar/']`
The `.zgroup` will be a case of the first condition `if '/' not in blob.name`, which if true, means that we have a blob, otherwise it's a directory. And the code in the else block removes the '/' from the directory.
So return value of `listdir('foo')` should be `['.zgroup', 'bar']`.

I ran into an issue desribed here https://herbsutter.com/gotw/_100/ .  Specifically: 

> You still need to write the visible class’ destructor yourself and define it out of line in the implementation file, even if normally it’s the same as what the compiler would generate. This is because although both unique_ptr and shared_ptr can be instantiated with an incomplete type, unique_ptr’s destructor requires a complete type in order to invoke delete (unlike shared_ptr which captures more information when it’s constructed). By writing it yourself in the implementation file, you force it to be defined in a place where impl is already defined, and this successfully prevents the compiler from trying to automatically generate the destructor on demand in the caller’s code where impl is not defined.

Oh I think there is a misunderstanding here. The NaN handling wasn't to remove the colors/elements with the NaNs in the Hue, that would remove entire colors from the colormap. It was meant to handle the case of grayscale colors where Hue results in NaN (done on purpose by me) and we need to take the previous hue. For example, if you had the color green which is a hue of like pi/3 and you needed to interpolate to the next color of white, the basic math of the algorithm could/would result in white having a hue of 0 (as a fallback for a `/ 0`). But we don't want hue to be interpolated between pi/3 and 0, we want hue to stay at pi/3 because when chroma is 0 hue has no effect.

The problem with my current approach though is that if we then interpolate from that white to blue (for example), we'd still be interpolating hue from pi/3 to pi instead of immediately jumping to pi. My guess/hope is that this happens for any grayscale color and that I can fix it. It may not help with the luminance/chroma issues you identified, but maybe it can clean up a couple other things.

Hm, I had some similar requirement in other applications where a file should be processed either from a path or an already open file. What I usually did was to use a single parameter like this:
```
source_path_or_io: Union[str, pathlib.Path, IO]
```
In case of `str` or `pathlib.Path` the function would open and close the file handle by itself. For everything else, it would just assume an open file-like object and just `read()` it. 

However, one issue arising from allowing file like objects in a single parameter is that not all of them have a proper `name` attribute. That prevents pygments from using the name as basis for figuring out the appropriate lexer.

So your approach with 2 parameters makes sense provided the file name can always be obtained and passed along in your own code calling this function.

I saw in your first kernel that you didn't pass the `Index64`'s `offset()` down anywhere. The `Index64` holds a shared pointer to its raw bytes. A simple slice does not copy those bytes; it just changes the `offset` (initially `0`) and `length` to the range we're considering, so that the sliced `Index64` is a view into the original one (sharing reference counting). This `offset` has to be explicitly passed into the kernel and used to get the correct values.

In general, you have to pass

```c++
index.ptr().get()   // pointer to the BASE of the array
index.offset()      // where this index really starts, relative to the base
index.length()      // the length of the index
```

into a kernel to use some `index`. In some cases, we've already verified that some lengths are equal to or less than others (especially `starts` and `stops`), so we might not pass all lengths down.

The kernels are a low-level interface, more like programming C than C++.

Well, RegularArray can't have a `size` of `0`: this is part of the specification in the [RegularArray documentation](https://awkward-array.readthedocs.io/en/latest/ak.layout.RegularArray.html) (see the `__init__` of the Python pseudocode). It's also prohibited by RegularArray's `is_valid` function:

```python
>>> import awkward as ak
>>> import numpy as np
>>> ak.Array(ak.layout.RegularArray(ak.layout.NumpyArray(np.array([1, 2, 3])), 0), check_valid=True)
ValueError: at layout (RegularArray): size < 1
```

This means that functions using RegularArrays freely assume that `size` is not `0`, so the consequences are unpredictable: it doesn't fail right away (though this validity constraint _could_, since `size` is just a scalar and checking it is _O(1)_...) but some operation on it might fail.

In looking for alternative representations that give you this desired behavior for `counts == 0`, I realized that we will have to support RegularArrays with `size == 0`. They can be made fairly naturally:

```python
>>> array1 = ak.Array([1, 2, 3, 4, 5])
>>> array2 = ak.Array([[1, 2, 3], [], [4, 5]])
>>> array1[np.newaxis][0:0, 0:0]
<Array [] type='0 * 0 * int64'>
>>> array2[np.newaxis][0:0, 0:0]
<Array [] type='0 * 0 * var * int64'>
```

but the second of these is a RegularArray with `size == 0`:

```python
>>> array2[np.newaxis][0:0, 0:0].layout
<RegularArray size="0">
    <content><ListArray64>
        <starts><Index64 i="[]" offset="0" length="0" at="0x000000000000"/></starts>
        <stops><Index64 i="[]" offset="0" length="0" at="0x000000000000"/></stops>
        <content><NumpyArray format="l" shape="5" data="1 2 3 4 5" at="0x5576b495c4b0"/></content>
    </ListArray64></content>
</RegularArray>
```

I'll open an issue to find all the places where this was assumed and relax the constraint. Go ahead and let `counts == 0`; the consequences will be dealt with in that other issue and PR.

I suspect `flake8` might be confused in this case. It has no problem with this code, for example:

```python3
def f(x):
    return 1 \
           + 2
```

In general, `flake8` doesn't seem to care whether you use `\` or brackets for line continuations. Probably because PEP 8 is a bit wishy-washy on it ("is preferred").

Looking [at the warning itself](https://lintlyci.github.io/Flake8Rules/rules/E127.html), it doesn't make any sense in this case. What it's saying is "hey, I think this code tries to visually align stuff, but it isn't correctly aligned". It's not saying "visual indent is bad". I misread it like that too.

I think it's a bad idea to change stuff just because some tool says so, unless you can think of a way in which it's bad. At that point, it doesn't matter if it was pointed out by a tool or not.

`flake8` and `pylint` (the more OCD one) and the like are nice helpers. Don't take them as dogma.

Breaking rules often makes sense.

This base class was actually derived from the `HSTCAL` class and made MORE general, which accounts for the additional options.  In short, the implementation in `ci_watson` provides FULL control over all available options for `FITSDiff` and `HDUDiff`, unlike the limited implementation used in `HSTCAL`.  The `HSTCAL` classes could, in priniciple, actually be based on the `ci_watson` base class with minimal or no changes.  In fact, I have plans to migrate all the `Drizzlepac` tests to use the `ci_watson` base class given here as is, work which will take place in Oct as part of the HLA in the Pipeline Project.  Does this serve to demonstrate its usefulness outside of `jwst`?  Providing this base class was intended to limit the number of implementations of common code useful for all missions regression tests (or at least, something that can be revised and maintained to support multiple missions).  

The initial implementation of `compare_outputs()` in `ci_watson.base_classes.BaseTest` also serves as the default implementation of the mechanism (already used by DrizzlePac) for OK'ing new test results.  It demonstrates how to set up and use the currently available function `generate_upload_schema()`.  However, the setup information for this function still needs to be turned into its own function which would be necessary in order to use the generate function properly. It should be possible to create a stand-alone function (or functions) to reproduce this behavior, but I would suggest that work wait until we actually have a use-case for the functional form as opposed to what is already working for all the JWST tests.

My PR was to fix the problem as soon as possible, as a dash is a common sign, but I have noted this down to investigate it further.

It seems that the tokenizer sepparate between "words" (returns `1`), "digits" (returns `0`, it can include `:`, etc.) and "no-words" (returns `2`) and, after that, it selects the valid tokens by discarding the "no words". However, those unexpected symbols return just an empty string and that breaks the filtering because it compares with 1 and gives the error you had. Fixing this should be as easy as changing this:

```
self.filtered_tokens = [t for t in self.tokens if t[1] <= 1]
```
by this:
```
self.filtered_tokens = [t for t in self.tokens if t[1] != '' and t[1] <= 1]
```

But I'm not sure of the implications and why it was decided to be implemented in this way...

I will do some more tests and push a "fixed" version.

We could give it a try, but this might be quite complex. This function does not perform any checks, it just prepares the request URI in the specification (f.e. `/2021-01-01/opensearch/domain/{DomainName}` for `delete_domain`) to be later matched against the incoming request.
This is necessary in order to determine which operation the request belongs to:
https://github.com/localstack/localstack/blob/86d48493931b810b8408dd8074b34a9d51d1f848/localstack/aws/protocol/parser.py#L518-L530
Afterwards we need the named capture groups in the regex to extract the path parameters from the path of the incoming request.

So, I definitely see the potential for the performance implication (especially about all the matches with the lookup using `next(filter(...))` in the snippet above), but the regular expressions are quite heavily used here. I am quite confident that we could increase the performance here by optimizing the path matching algorithm, but it seemed like an overkill for the first iteration.

In this particular case, I can empathise with the temptation to avoid using Angular's Dependency Injection (DI). Why you might think this way:
1. force_svg_util itself currently has no DI-able dependencies (although I think it's hard to be certain that will be true forever)
2. we don't foresee wanting to to DI some other implementation of ForceSVGDataSource -- we think it will always be a local storage thing, unlike persistent settings which might one day be stored on the server.

Having said that, I think it's good habit/hygiene to make this a DI-able object like any other object in our system that reads/writes to a data source. I agree there is some overhead but it is pretty standard Angular stuff. It will also give you standard options/patterns when you write tests - how to Mock, how to Fake/Stub.

Ah the failing tests actually overwrite the files. The logic is as follows:

1. If a test fails, create directory if it doesn't exist
2. If header doesn't exist, create header file
3. Create summary file for this test, potentially overwriting previous existing file
4. If the test then succeeds in a second or third trial, remove the summary test file

Thus, when a test succeeds after it failed, the summary is always removed. That works and that is also the reason why we currently see these "empty" summaries: The test summary files have all been removed, but the header still exists, so the header is printed.

With this PR, we will only upload annotations if the header and at least one test summary file are present (so 2 files).

In #1306, `pinList` was converted into a repeated capability. Since we can't have two unrelated repeated capabilities in the same method, we need to choose one over the other. I think we should choose `pinList` and revert `site` to being a parameter. Two reasons for this:
- API expects `site` in this method to represent exactly one site. Usability gain from making `sites` a repeated capability is pretty low.
- #1301 updates `get_pattern_pin_names` to return a `list` of `str`. Users typically would want use the output from that method for the `pin_list` in this method, which they can do easily if `pins` is a repeated capability. Else, they'll have to convert `list` of `str` into single `str`.

If we decide to keep `site` as a parameter, we should do the same for other HRAM methods, for the sake of consistency -  `FetchHistoryRAMCycleInformation` and `FetchHistoryRAMScanCycleNumber`.

Also, it would be nice if users can pass `site` as an int rather than as `str` formatted as `siteN`. I'm guessing we can do this with a simple converter. This should be done in a separate PR (please open an issue, if we decide to keep `site` as a parameter).

The intention's to go into the channel specific content metadata exporter and use the defined mappings to tailor and sanitize the code (see https://github.com/openedx/edx-enterprise/blob/master/integrated_channels/sap_success_factors/exporters/content_metadata.py). There we have two different types of mapping methods, `transform_{attribute}` and `transform_{content_type}_{attribute}` because some external platforms care about and want to distinguish between metadata depending on what type of content it is, ie SAP wants different titles for course runs than for course and programs. In this case we're supporting the old implementation while also allowing for the caller to specify more detail/actions.  `transform_{content_type}_{attribute}` isn't currently being used by any channel yet but I wanted to add the functionality as we know channels do this content specific mapping already and we may want to support it in an upgrade in the future.

In any case to be coherent with conventions and queries in general: - `get_adviser_run_origins_all` should retrieve all origins in adviser run, then filters can be applied: 

- `package_name`, `package_version`, `index_url`, `distinct` also should be there because if we want to count how many for a specific origin we can reuse logic across queries. 

- `start_offset` and `count` should be considered if we want to consider pagination purposes.

Moreover `package_name` and `package_version` should be used with normalize method:

`package_name = self.normalize_python_package_name(package_name)`
`package_version = self.normalize_python_package_version(package_version)`

What about?

```
    def get_adviser_run_origins_all(
        self,
        *,
        package_name: str = None,
        package_version: str = None,
        index_url: str = None,
        start_offset: int = 0,
        count: Optional[int] = DEFAULT_COUNT,
        distinct: bool = False,
    ) -> List[str]:
        """Retrieve all origins (git repos URLs) in Adviser Run.

        Examples:
        >>> from thoth.storages import GraphDatabase
        >>> graph = GraphDatabase()
        >>> graph.get_adviser_run_origins_all()
        [HERE SHOULD GO AN EXAMPLE OF A RESULT]
        """
        with self._session_scope() as session:

            query = (
                session.query(AdviserRun)
                .order_by(AdviserRun.origin)
                .order_by(AdviserRun.datetime.desc())
            )

            if package_version or package_name:
                query = (
                    query.join(PythonRequirementsLock, PythonRequirementsLock.python_software_stack_id == AdviserRun.user_software_stack_id)
                    .join(PythonPackageVersion)
                )

                if index_url:
                    query = query.join(PythonPackageIndex)

            query = query.with_entities(AdviserRun.origin)

            if package_name is not None:
                package_name = self.normalize_python_package_name(package_name)
                query.filter(PythonPackageVersion.package_name == package_name)

            if package_version is not None:
                package_version = self.normalize_python_package_version(package_version)
                query.filter(PythonPackageVersion.package_version == package_version)

            if index_url is not None:
                query.filter(PythonPackageIndex.url  == index_url)

            query = query.offset(start_offset).limit(count)

            if distinct:
                query = query.distinct()

            result = [r[0] for r in query.all() if r[0]] # We do not consider None results

            return result
```

`default_list=[]` in the function header is the same data structure globally - across all `MachineStack` objects. An example to show the dependencies across objects:
```python
In [3]: class Foo(object):
   ...:     def __init__(self, default_list=[]):
   ...:         self.my_list = default_list
   ...:     def append(self, v):
   ...:         self.my_list.append(v)
   ...:         
In [4]: f = Foo()

In [5]: f.append(42)

In [6]: f.my_list
Out[6]: [42]

In [7]: f2 = Foo()

In [8]: f2.append(1337)

In [9]: f2.my_list
Out[9]: [42, 1337]
```

As you can see, even though we have two separate instances, the state of `f` is dragged into `f2`, because the default arguments of `Foo.__init__` is evaluated only once - when the class is defined. Basically all instances of `Foo` work on the same list pointer. So it's not a problem limited to the constructor, but to everything that touches `default_list` directly or indirectly.

If the answer is we should, then we need to define a clear way for distinguishing inputs apart from name on API level, instead of the undocumented DFS order.

```
{"foo": {"secure": "bar"}}
```

`Value(NopDecrypter)` will return the literal text, while `Value(NewBlindingDecrypter())` will replace the entire nested secure string with `[secret]`:

```
{"foo": "[secret]"}

In urllib3 sometimes one single job fails because of a flaky test and we have no idea if coverage is still at 100% or not. The test suite will still be failing, but that way we don't need to wait for everything to pass to know if coverage is still at 100%. It will also cover the case when as admins we just merge a change even if unrelated tests are failing: we have merged uncovered code in the past like that.

>What would mocking the converter get us?
 
Our Python APIs accept all sorts of things for repeated capabilities:
* `0`
* `[0,1]`
* `"0,1"`
* etc... 

We know the conversion is done by a single function so this test only tests one type of conversion. In essence, the test assumes that if one conversion worked, all of them will because they go through the one function. Which is true today but there is guarantee that it will be true in the future.

So we could either stop assuming it goes through that one function by checking for it explicitly through a mock (test the implementation, not great) or we can test more types (test the behavior, arguably more correct).

In what context are you discussing here? Because I've tested this script against several pySigma backends and they all generate valid queries:

```
sigma convert -t splunk okta_password_in_alternateid_field.yml --without-pipeline
Parsing Sigma rules  [####################################]  100%
legacyeventtype="core.user_auth.login_failed"  NOT (actor_alternateid IN ("0oa*", "*@okta.com"))
| regex actor_alternateid=".*[a-z].*"
| regex actor_alternateid=".*[A-Z].*"
| regex actor_alternateid=".*\\d.*"
| regex actor_alternateid=".*[\\\"!\\#\\$%\\&'’\\(\\)\\*\\+,\\-\\./\\\\:;<=>\\?@\\[\\]\\^_`\\{\\|\\}\\~].*"
```

```
sigma convert -t loki okta_password_in_alternateid_field.yml 
Parsing Sigma rules  [####################################]  100%
{job=~".+"} | logfmt | legacyeventtype=`core.user_auth.login_failed` and actor_alternateid=~`.*[a-z].*` and actor_alternateid=~`.*[A-Z].*` and actor_alternateid=~`.*\d.*` and actor_alternateid=~".*[\\\"!\\#\\$%\\&'’\\(\\)\\*\\+,\\-\\./\\\\:;<=>\\?@\\[\\]\\^_`\\{\\|\\}\\~].*" and actor_alternateid!~`(?i)0oa.*` and actor_alternateid!~`(?i).*@okta\.com`
```

The alternate approach to such a valid password regex, used by the original blogpost, was using zero-width assertions: `(?=.*\d)(?=.*[a-z])(?=.*[A-Z])` - but that's using a regex feature that not all backends will necessarily support (I know Loki doesn't).

However, I do understand both your concerns about the requirement for tuning to a specific environment. @phantinuss yes, the _default_ format for [Okta usernames is an email address](https://help.okta.com/en-us/Content/Topics/users-groups-profiles/usgp-create-character-restriction.htm) - so I could go with something along the lines of filtering out `[A-Z0-9._%+-]+@[A-Z0-9.-]+\.[A-Z]{2,4}` perhaps? Misses edge cases of some newer domains (i.e., those that are longer than 4 characters), but should be valid for most configurations.

I'd be happy to move this rule into the `rule-placeholder` folder if it fits there better. If we go down that route, would we have to have a single regex placeholder for the valid usernames?

pack_message does not assume the type of encoding that will be used by the consumer because it doesn't assume the transport layer used. For example, in an HTTP Request, a JSON object can be put directly into the body of the request. However, if the transport layer is a URL then it should be base64URL encoded. However, in unpack_message it can be assumed that the API consumer will be processing the JSON object returned. In this case, we are assuming that the language will process JSON based on a specific type (string) in which case we can handle the encoding.

We've discussed this topic before (in slack) and also here in GitHub, in an [issue](https://github.com/SigmaHQ/sigma/pull/1249#issuecomment-754862329):

>> @thomaspatzke: I expect a huge amount of FPs
>
> @yugoslavskiy: You are right. There is no way to differentiate the legitimate execution of find/ls from malicious use. So it is a behavior detection with low confidence. As there is no such metric in Sigma (at least, for now), I've used `level: low`. When you do `from django.conf import settings` - the `settings` object is not actually the direct settings, but a Django `LazySettings` object. As soon as you try to access a key from the settings object, it will check that it is initialized, if it is not, it will throw an error. That's why I was confused!

What will probably be good in the future is actually to move our logging configuration out of the settings `base.py` and into a separate file so that we can reference it independently of the settings (and also avoid the repetition in `options.py` too). This will have the slight issue of causing problems if someone completely overrides our base settings, but that's their problem, not ours!
Might be worth noting that this change only works because of the `encapsulated_logger` in `DaemonPantsRunner`, and therefore we don't have to gate logging setup anymore.

> obj.content_encoding is set according to result

False, it is not set according to `result`. It is set according to `self.serializer`, which is always the same.

Kombu `dumps()` function would only take `result` into account if `self.serializer` were `None` or `"raw"`. But this is not possible because celery's allowed serializers are only: json, pickle, yaml and msgpack (or custom serializer, but not None nor "raw").

I'd even go further and change the `log_recv()` function too, just in case the target answers with non ascii formatted data.

I did some testing to see what the difference between those two methods are.
`decode()` will interpret all input including line breaks. The result of this is, that the scv file will have line breaks inside the data field. This seems to be allowed as long as the field is surrounded by quotation marks, which it currently is.
But then we have the problem that quotation marks inside the data stream will mess with this so I don't think it's a good idea to decode the data.
On the pro side, decoding the data does of course improve the readability greatly, but `repr()` seems to be the better choice to guarantee compatibility.

Using `get_or_create` with `save` will invoke database 3 times in a case where a user does not exist: 

1. first a query to check if it exists, 
2. then a creation if it does not, and
3. finally a save round to update the counters and metadata.

The old logic only uses two database invocations at most, and this MR potentially increases the database query load by 50% per attempt for cases where an access attempt does not previously exist?

- https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/3.2/ref/models/querysets/#get-or-create
- https://github.com/django/django/blob/ca9872905559026af82000e46cde6f7dedc897b6/django/db/models/query.py#L571

The proposed logic does avoid a race condition in case where the [database is not configured for atomic requests with `ATOMIC_REQUESTS = True`](https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/3.2/ref/settings/#std:setting-DATABASE-ATOMIC_REQUESTS) on the engine level AND the unique fields are defined on the model, but this comes at a cost here. Putting a dashboard with the same name for each instance ID doesn't seem desirable. Currently there is only one dashboard that can be referenced from config, so that implies one CloudWatch dashboard per Ray cluster. 

Having a single dashboard for the entire cluster also precludes referencing instance IDs within the dashboard config, since calls to put_dashboard doesn't take the symmetric difference of widgets between two calls, but always replaces the dashboards widgets with those specified in the latest call.

I see a few ways to resolve this issue:
1) Only support one dashboard for each cluster initially, and remove {instance_id} variable support from it.
2) Support a set of dashboard configs for the cluster, allowing both the dashboard name and config to reference per-EC2-instance variables.

I think it's probably ok to start with support for (1) in this PR, and then follow-up with an appropriate design for (2) in a subsequent PR.

It's being cautious, really, but there is an extra feature that can be useful: if `auditwheel repair` detects that your wheel is backward compatible with older platforms, it will add the relevant tag to the final wheel.

For instance, if you were to compile the wheel inside the `manylinux_2_24` docker image, at the end you'd get a file called `wrapt-1.12.2-cp39-cp39-linux_x86_64.whl`, which PyPI would reject on upload because it doesn't accept the `linux` platform tag. Since you know you were in `manylinux_2_24`, you could just rename it to `wrapt-1.12.2-cp39-cp39-manylinux_2_24_x86_64.whl` and upload it like that.

However, here's what happens if you run `auditwheel repair` instead:
```
INFO:auditwheel.main_repair:Repairing wrapt-1.12.2-cp39-cp39-linux_x86_64.whl
INFO:auditwheel.main_repair:Wheel is eligible for a higher priority tag. You requested manylinux_2_24_x86_64 but I have found this wheel is eligible for manylinux_2_5_x86_64.
INFO:auditwheel.wheeltools:Previous filename tags: linux_x86_64
INFO:auditwheel.wheeltools:New filename tags: manylinux_2_5_x86_64, manylinux1_x86_64, manylinux_2_24_x86_64
INFO:auditwheel.wheeltools:Previous WHEEL info tags: cp39-cp39-linux_x86_64
INFO:auditwheel.wheeltools:New WHEEL info tags: cp39-cp39-manylinux_2_5_x86_64, cp39-cp39-manylinux1_x86_64, cp39-cp39-manylinux_2_24_x86_64
```

It basically detected that the library was not linking against anything, and that it could even be tagged `manylinux1`, the oldest `manylinux` environment, meaning that virtually anybody could install that wheel now.

This isn't a big deal right now, but if for some reason we're caching large async responses we could end up with a large cache that doesn't get cleared out, so I'd rather have some kind of bound on the size of the cache. Will compromise by only sending the ack on every 10th response, but that can be adjusted.

Also, I'm not actually sure how much extra overhead it would be, since the stream is constantly open anyway. If every 10th is still too much overhead, we can always adjust down the line.

New non-detection models don't use this idiom any more (returning different output depending on jit/training flag), so I think it's safer to handle it explicitly.

I tried to do 2 delegates but it was messy. Because delegates can't do any implicit conversions i required us to have overloads everywhere for sync & async delegates, as well as overloads for `TStack` generic being provided. This was tolerable but still verbose on things like `InlineProgramArgs` where an inline program is required, but it was downright messy on something like `UpOptions` where an inline program was optional - as that required either: 1. sticking with a single async property and requiring the consumer to return a task or 2. having 2 conflicting properties, sync & async, and making sure the consumer only used 1.

Instead I tried changing the `PulumiFn` to a class. This can take in any number of delegates, including a `TStack`. So anywhere we take in `PulumiFn` we now support `TStack`. And it is very easy for us to change the shapes of any delegates that we want to allow as those changes will be encapsulated within `PulumiFn` and won't affect our other APIs since the overloads are no longer necessary.

I removed our dependency on `arrow` and switched everything over to `ciso8601` which returns "real" Python `datetime` objects, so while I don't expect subclasses of `datetime` to appear, I don't think we necessarily need to prevent them either.

The reason we check for exact type equality in the `date` case is to prevent implicit loss of precision: since `datetime` is technically a subclass of `date`, `isinstance(some_datetime, date)` would return true but when it's used as a date, the time and time zone components would be implicitly lost. In the opposite direction, there is no associated loss of precision (the conversion is widening, not narrowing), so this is not really a concern. Additionally, `isinstance(some_date, datetime)` returns `False`, which is nice and as expected 😄

When yield returns control to this method, you can't assume the step has actually finished running.  You only know that the step is finished when the callback has been called.

I suggest setting `processStatus = ""` before doing `yield from self.step.job(...)`

```
self.processStatus = ""
yield from self.step.job(...)
while self.processStatus == "":
  yield None
```

The loop callback needs to always set `self.processStatus = processStatus` to indicate the callback was called and the loop should proceed.

There are many cases where the user will want to keep their checkpoints in a particular location.  Needing to provide two separate callbacks that do the same thing in different directories is a bad user experience.

If you want to support the user not having to specify the path (because they don't want to keep the checkpoint files around), then you can solve this by creating a subclass of `ModelCheckpoint` for this purpose:

```
KerasEstimator(checkpoint_callback=hvd.callbacks.ReturnedModelCheckpoint(
    monitor='val_acc',
    mode='max',
    save_best_only=True), ...)
```

Nice thing about this approach is (1) it's flexible if the user wants to provide an alternative location to write checkpoints, and (2) you can prevent them from setting unnecessary things like `filepath` and `save_weights_only`.

Even though I think we don't explicitly mention it in our style guide, in terms of method names, I like that we start the name with a "verb/action" (I feel it reads better from the caller side - i.e., it's clear what action we are performing on the object). 

The only exception is `To{SomeType}()`, even then in some cases I actually prefer using `ConvertTo{SomeType}()` name. 

Here is my suggestion for this:
- We use `To{SomeType}()`name in cases the method converts and then returns the given type (like `ToString()` which returns a `String` instance).
- We use `ConvertTo{SomeType}(SomeType &)` method name in cases where the method convertsto a new type and outputs the converted value in a passed-in reference parameter.
- i.e.,
```
class Type
{
    OtherType ToOtherType(void) const;                    // First option (preferred)     
    void ConvertToOtherType(OtherType &aOtherType) const; // Second option (can return `otError` if conversion can fail)
    
    void ToOtherType(OtherType &aOtherType) const;        // Third option (not preferred).
};
```

`mRegisterCurrentChildIndex` is set to True when `kDuaReRegister` is received from PBBR. 
However, the Child might update its addresses after `kDuaReRegister` is received but before next Dua registration. 

For example, after `kDuaReRegister` is received and `mRegisterCurrentChildIndex` set to true, the Child could remove its DUA and update new addresses. So, when the Parent tries to reregister for this Child, the Child does not have any Dua, causing this function to exit with `error = OT_ERROR_ABORT `. In such case, other Children might have Dua to register but couldn't get the chance. 

Thus I have this suggestion:
```suggestion
        if (!mRegisterCurrentChildIndex && IsChildHasDuaToRegister(mChildIndexDuaRegistering))
```
Where `IsChildHasDuaToRegister` is to be implemented.

Answering your previous comment:

> > While if the object is only defined in other "user" modules (except **main**), it may be fair to save it as a reference to one of them (cases 4 and 5, builtin), or alternatively to raise an error too. This last case is the single one I'm not sure of how it should behave.
> 
> I'd say, since it's `refonfail=True`... then this makes sense. I'm wondering if there's a case where it fails in `mod` and is then saved by reference to `other.name` or similar... but the object is actually defined in `mod`, and `other.name` is just a reference to the original in `mod`. The question is: is it possible for this to go wrong... where save-by-reference to `other.name` is the wrong thing to do. I think we need to try to think up failure scenarios for this case.

This problem of saving a reference to an object of other module that we don't know where was defined is the same that already existed for the `refimported` option (the old `byref` for modules). It could go wrong in many cases, yes, although I think they are a small minority of cases.

What I could think of to minimize this was: get the module associated with the object. It may be `obj.__module__` or `type(obj).__module__`. If it matches the module being pickled, don't save it as a reference to other modules, as it's most likely that the object was defined in the module itself. Secondly, if the module being pickled is part of a package, and the module associated with the object is from this package, only save it as a reference to a module of the same package.

The reason for the second rule is that packages in general are independent and have a unidirectional relationship, i.e. one acts as a library and the other as a client. So, suppose we are pickling a module `pack1.bar`, and we hit an unpickleable object of type `pack1.baz.Class`. We then find the same object also defined in a module `pack2.foo`. The most probable scenario is that `pack2` uses `pack1` as a library and `pack2.foo` imported the object from `pack1.bar` (the module we are pickling), not the opposite. The alternative hypothesis is that `pack2.foo` used `pack1.baz.Class` to create the object and then injected it in the `pack1.bar` namespace, which is very unlikely.

Logging handlers are mainly used to write logs to different sources. By default handlers acquire an [i/o lock](https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/3.11/Lib/logging/__init__.py#L965) before handling a log. This is expensive and is not necessary to intercept and store log messages for the telemetry writer. Instead of using a stream or file handler I could implement the [nullhandler](https://docs.python.org/3/library/logging.handlers.html#nullhandler) but this is not supported in python 2.7. I could override some of this logic in the file/stream handlers to improve performance but I don't think it's worth the effort.

Another approach could be to define a telemetry log filter. One limitation of this approach is that log filters are not automatically applied to descendant loggers. Ensuring the filter is configured correctly could be a pain.

> Note that filters attached to handlers are consulted before an event is emitted by the handler, whereas filters attached to loggers are consulted whenever an event is logged (using [debug()](https://docs.python.org/3/library/logging.html#logging.debug), [info()](https://docs.python.org/3/library/logging.html#logging.info), etc.), before sending an event to handlers. This means that events which have been generated by descendant loggers will not be filtered by a logger’s filter setting, unless the filter has also been applied to those descendant loggers. localBackendReference held onto localBackend only to look up the current project, so it could shorten the `String()` representation. I figured it can do that with the name of the current project at creation time instead.

```
[
    (2, 1, 6, '', ('0.0.0.0', 8080)),
    (10, 1, 6, '', ('::', 8080, 0, 0))
]
```

Where `'0.0.0.0'` and `'::'` are IPs.

> I'm assuming (out of sheer ignorance) that it is too late for me to create a branch etc?

It's never too late. Except maybe for this specific pull request. But since elsewhere, I've recommended to step back and approach the problem differently, it will be a good opportunity to work with branches. Here's what I'd recommend (I'm going to assume you're using a command-line client, but you can find similar operations in a GUI client):

1. First, you want to save the work you've already done in this PR (in your main branch) by giving it a branch name. Make sure you've committed any changes you want to keep or discard any changes you do not. Then, while still on your main (with the latest changes pulled), just run `git branch feature/windows-shard-pr544`. That will create a new branch with its head pointing to the same commits as your current main. That's not normally how one creates a branch, but it's a good way to create a branch without checking out that branch.
2. Since you're still on `main`, reset it to the state of upstream. If you don't already have an `upstream` remote configured, you should configure one with `git remote add upstream https://github.com/jaraco/keyring`. Then make sure you have the commits from upstream with `git fetch upstream`. Then reset main with `git reset --hard upstream/main`.
3. Now you're ready to create a new branch for new development. Use `git checkout -b feature/windows-shard-take-2` (you can call the branch whatever you want; I just suggest names like I might use).
4. Finally, after you've committed some changes to your branch and you want to create a pull request, you first need to push that branch to your fork. Assuming your fork is the "origin", while on the branch, you do that with `git push --set-upstream origin` (I think; you may also need to pass the name of the branch).

I know this branching stuff can get confusing, so I do recommend grabbing an SCM visualization GUI like Sourcetree (free) or Sublime Merge ($) or PyCharm ($). It helps me visualize the changes that are happening to different branches of development and their state across local and remote repos. If you love the command line, you may also be interested in the [gh tool](https://github.com/cli/cli) as it helps facilitate a lot of github operations (such as creating pull requests) from the command-line.

Hope that helps you get started on your next branch!

The `Construct` adapter func in this PR adapts the raw gRPC interface to/from the Pulumi Go SDK model.

https://github.com/pulumi/pulumi/blob/a8ea363df970c58709097b3ab28e82afc5a0d9ce/sdk/proto/go/provider.pb.go#L2047-L2048

So the provider in this file implements the raw gRPC interface.

To use `plugin.providerServer`, `Construct` would have to be an adapter of `plugin.providerServer`'s interface:

https://github.com/pulumi/pulumi/blob/664776efe85ca4c6a8c9cd95fa2fbfa75f2dbf03/sdk/go/common/resource/plugin/provider.go#L78-L80

Should `Construct` be an adapter for `plugin.providerServer` instead? Or should we have adapters for both the raw gRPC interface **and** `plugin.providerServer`?

The reason I made `Construct` an adapter for the raw gRPC interface is because 1) our existing [provider boilerplate repo](https://github.com/pulumi/pulumi-provider-boilerplate) implements a provider using the raw gRPC interface rather than `plugin.providerServer` and 2) our own native providers also implement the raw gRPC interface over `plugin.providerServer`.

So chunk_info is holding a shared_ptr to the underlying PlasmaBuffer, so it seems that the memcpy should be safe as long as the chunk_info above is in scope.

I'm wondering if this is actually a new bug introduced by this fix: https://github.com/ray-project/ray/pull/16620/files

Here's my understanding:
- There are two refs to the underlying allocation, one from the PlasmaBuffer itself (decremented on destruction), and another from the client.
- AbortCreate() is (1) calling Release(), then (2) Abort(), then create_buffer_state.erase(object_id).
- Prior to 16620, by step (2) the ref-count drops to zero and plasma server can delete the object safely.
- But after 16620, there may be remaining shared_ptr refs to the PlasmaBuffer for the receive. Hence, we could get a Release() call for an already-deleted object.

If the above is correct, then this fix in this PR may be incomplete: you probably also want to remove the shared_ptr from ChunkInfo.
